U.S. Oil Recovery Threatened by Lower Prices

U.S. oil production accelerated to a record rate after the war with Iran and closure of the Strait of Hormuz boosted prices and dispelled some of the gloom hanging over the industry at the end of 2025.

Total production of crude and condensates accelerated to 13.9 million barrels per day in April, surpassing the previous record set six months earlier in October 2025.

In the short term, faster completions, unchoking existing wells and more production from stripper wells have all probably boosted output, but a more sustained increase requires more drilling ...

China’s Private Refiners Snap Up Middle East Oil as Prices Slide

China’s independent refiners are taking advantage of cheaper Middle Eastern oil after flows accelerated through the Strait of Hormuz, snapping up barrels from producers including Saudi Arabia and Iraq.

Rongsheng Petrochemical Co. has bought Saudi crude on a spot basis for arrival in July, while Shandong Chambroad Petrochemicals Co. purchased Iraq’s Basrah grade for August, according to traders with knowledge of the matter. Another processor, Shenghong Petrochemical Group Co., acquired Upper Zakum from the United Arab Emirates, they said.

Refiners in Asia have been inundated with offers as shipments through Hormuz picked up following an interim peace deal between Washington and Tehran, and the US issued a temporary waiver allowing the purchase of Iranian crude. State producers Saudi Aramco and Abu Dhabi National Oil Co. have been offering their oil on more flexible terms in an effort to encourage higher sales.

Representatives from Rongsheng, Chambroad, and Shenghong didn’t immediately respond to emails seeking comment.

Many of the cargoes sold by Aramco and Iraq were priced at discounts of as much as $5 a barrel to Brent futures on a delivered basis to China, said the traders, asking to not be identified discussing private information.

Those levels are much cheaper than grades from West Africa and Brazil, which are the typical sources for their spot crude requirements, due to costly long-haul freight, traders said. It’s also comparable to, or possibly cheaper than recent offers for Iranian crude, they added.

Key takeaways from EIA’s weekly report:
Crude stocks fell for a 12th straight week, down 3.8 million barrels to their lowest since March 2025. Including the SPR, which fell by 5.5 million barrels, total stockpiles dropped to their lowest since 2004. Cushing recorded a small increase but remains at a seasonal low last seen in 2005. Both imports and exports slowed, while production held steady at 13.8 million barrels per day.

Gulf Coast refinery activity rose to a seasonal record high, yet refinery margins remain elevated amid strong demand. Gasoline stocks fell by 2.3 million barrels as exports hit a seasonal record high, while domestic demand firmed on a four-week average basis as the summer driving season picked up. Distillate stocks continued to recover, rising by 2.5 million barrels.

Overall, total US crude oil and refined fuel exports continued their sharp decline, falling to 11.2 million barrels per day last week from more than 14 million barrels per day in April. The sustained drop reflects softer international demand and reduced export flows following the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, allowing Middle Eastern supplies to regain market share.

#Commodities

#Gold jumped on Wednesday, briefly trading above the key USD 4,100 level after rebounding from an intraday low of USD 3,960, as Fed Chair Warsh dampened speculation that the Fed may raise interest rates this year, saying inflation risks have eased in recent weeks alongside lower energy prices. Meanwhile, the one-year US inflation swap has fallen sharply from a May peak of 3.5% to around 2.1%, reinforcing expectations that inflation concerns may gradually fade and potentially helping establish a floor under precious metals. Ahead of Friday's US holiday, the market's focus turns to the US jobs report, the dollar, and Treasury yields.

Brent #crudeoil extended its slide towards USD 70 and pre-war levels as flows through the Strait of Hormuz continued to recover, while signs of progress in indirect US-Iran talks further eased supply concerns. Combined with ongoing releases from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve and demand that has been curtailed by recent high prices, the market remains exposed to a short-term supply glut despite the broader market remaining relatively tight. Refined fuel products continue to trade strongly amid constrained supplies, keeping refinery margins elevated and delaying the pass-through of lower crude prices to consumers.

South32 sells aluminium business to Alcoa in $9.8b deal

Alcoa's president and CEO William Oplinger says a deal to acquire South32's alumina assets will generate $US900m worth of synergies. The $US5.6 billion ($8.1 billion) deal includes South32's bauxite mines, smelters and refineries in Western Australia, Brazil and South Africa. Oplinger says the deal is a "great strategic fit" for Alcoa, and he has downplayed fears that it will result in job losses in WA. South32's new CEO Matt Daley says the deal will allow the company to focus on its copper and zinc operations. However, it still owns the mothballed Mozal aluminium smelter in Mozambique, which it is keen to sell. (RMS)

Brent: surplus spooks the bulls

• Goldman Sachs expects an oil market surplus of 3 million BPD in 2027.

• Without Warsh’s help, Japan will be forced to resort to currency interventions.

The Dollar is consolidating ahead of Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra, Portugal, and the release of key US labour market data. A hawkish surprise from the new Fed Chair at the June FOMC meeting sent financial markets into a tailspin, leading to a rise in Treasury yields and the greenback. If the central bank chief hints that he was misunderstood, everything will be turned on its head.

Meanwhile, the strengthening of the dollar has been one of the reasons behind the yen’s fall to 40-year lows and Brent’s rapid return to pre-Middle East conflict levels. In the spring, the rise in oil prices drove the dollar higher, but now the strengthening dollar is putting downward pressure on oil prices. That said, there are already plenty of headwinds for ‘black gold’.

Since the deal with the US was concluded, Iran has exported around 40 million barrels. Coupled with the rise in Russian oil supplies to 4.13M BPD, the highest since the beginning of 2022, this is exerting significant downward pressure on Brent.

Despite flare-ups in the conflict in the Middle East, tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues. The resumption of operations there allows Goldman Sachs to forecast a 3M BPD surplus in the oil market by 2027. Of this, 1 million BPD will be used to replenish strategic reserves. However, the remaining figure will be sufficient to give crude oil bears confidence.

The only thing likely to unsettle them would be the resumption of large-scale US air strikes against Iran. Donald Trump has not ruled out such action, but favours diplomacy.

Meanwhile, the USDJPY has exceeded 162 for the first time since 1986. Investors are bracing for a resumption of currency interventions, on which Japan spent over $70 billion in April and May. According to Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, the government and the central bank are ready to take appropriate measures regarding currencies at any time as necessary. The official noted that her US counterpart, Scott Bessent, had agreed with this.

If Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra fails to halt the US dollar’s advance, Japan will have no choice but to intervene in the forex market.

Summary: A strong dollar and rising supply are weighing on Brent; Japan is preparing to intervene amid a fall in the yen. (FxPro)

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

Now that this threat has been removed, it is having a positive impact on the USD index.

The strengthening of the US dollar has created headwinds for gold, pushing it back below $4,000 per ounce. While the fall in oil prices is reducing inflationary risks, the market remains fixated on the possibility of a Fed rate hike and a stronger dollar.

Precious metals have seen a brief resurgence amid a reduced likelihood of monetary tightening. The probability of monetary tightening in September has fallen over the past week from over 70% to 62%, whilst the likelihood of two federal funds rate hikes in 2026 has dropped from 50% to 38%. Nevertheless, investors’ attention is focused more on the persistence of relatively high interest rates, causing non-yielding gold to lose ground.

The high yields on Treasuries are being sustained by Warsh’s emphasis on the Fed talking less and acting more. The new chair wants the markets to signal to the central bank where rates should be, rather than the central bank dictating to the markets. The Fed’s shift from verbosity to reticence is heightening uncertainty and allowing investors to demand a higher risk premium. This is reflected in debt market rates and the dynamics of Gold.

The bulls believe that gold will prove more resilient to headwinds. The bears are counting on it falling further towards $3,600 before buying interest returns.

Summary: Gold is retreating under pressure from a strengthening dollar and expectations of high Fed interest rates; bears are anticipating a fall to $3,600 before demand returns. (FxPro)

The dollar: geopolitics back in the picture

The escalation of the conflict in the Middle East will bolster the greenback as a safe-haven asset

Data on European inflation and the US labour market will determine the fate of EURUSD

The US dollar quickly recouped its losses after the US resumed bombing Iran in response to its attack on tankers in the Strait of Hormuz. Prior to this, the greenback had been falling amid profit-taking on long positions due to reduced expectations of aggressive monetary tightening by the Fed. The rise in the University of Michigan’s Consumer Sentiment Index in June, coupled with a slowdown in long-term inflation expectations, suggests that two Fed rate rises in 2026 may be avoided.

According to Morgan Stanley, EURUSD could easily reach 1.1, as long-term investors are gradually unwinding their long positions whilst hedge funds are building up short positions. BNY Mellon sees potential for a fall below this level, as the ECB is unlikely to raise rates further due to the negative impact of monetary tightening on the eurozone economy.

The Fed’s ‘hawkish’ shift and Christine Lagarde’s ‘dovish’ rhetoric have sent EURUSD tumbling to annual lows. Investors will be looking for confirmation of the downtrend in data on European inflation and the US labour market. Bloomberg experts expect consumer prices in the eurozone to slow from 3.2% to 3% in June. If inflation is indeed peaking, there is no need to tighten monetary policy. This is bad news for the euro.

The improvement in employment trends this spring provided a reason to keep the federal funds rate on hold. Further stabilisation of the labour market will allow the Fed to raise it as inflation picks up. Divergence in monetary policy will continue to support the ‘bears’ on EURUSD. If, at the same time, oil prices start to rise due to tensions in the Middle East, the euro risks falling to $1.1.

Brent continues to react to good news and turn a blind eye to the bad. Rumours of a ceasefire and negotiations have had a greater impact on the North Sea crude than the resumption of Tehran’s attacks on tankers and US airstrikes on Iran. However, if fears continue to hamper traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and slow the process of restoring oil production in the Persian Gulf to pre-war levels, prices will soar. This will support the US dollar as the currency of a net exporter of energy commodities. (FxPro)

$4,000: Just a pause, not the end of Gold's decline

Gold is rebounding at the end of the week after a correction in the odds of two key Fed rate hikes this year. Nevertheless, the price is falling for the fourth consecutive week. Since the second half of the week, bears have been relentlessly trying to push the price below the psychologically significant $4,000 mark but eased their grip on Friday afternoon. At the same time, the long-term moving averages favour sellers, as a ‘death cross’ is looming (when the 50-day moving average falls below the 200-day moving average), reinforced by the fact that the price is trading below this cross. On the weekly charts, attempts to push the price back above the 50-week moving average have failed. However, this is also the price support zone from the end of last year, so we can expect a fairly fierce battle around the current level.

It is also worth noting how the narrative for gold’s price movements has changed. Previously, the escalation between the US and Iran triggered downward price movements, with a clear inverse correlation between oil and gold. But it has now become apparent that both assets can move in the same direction – downwards.

However, we must also consider an alternative scenario, in which, since the end of January, the price of gold has been correcting following three years of growth, reaching the 61.8% level—a classic Fibonacci support level. Under this scenario, we should see consolidation followed by a reversal to the upside, with the first stage being a recovery to peak levels around $5,600, and the second stage a rally to $8,000 as part of an extension to the 161.8% level. We nevertheless view this undoubtedly inspiring scenario as less likely, given the history of previous similar price booms and crashes over the last half-century. (FxPro)

News

Brent: surplus spooks the bulls

• Goldman Sachs expects an oil market surplus of 3 million BPD in 2027.

• Without Warsh’s help, Japan will be forced to resort to currency interventions.

The Dollar is consolidating ahead of Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra, Portugal, and the release of key US labour market data. A hawkish surprise from the new Fed Chair at the June FOMC meeting sent financial markets into a tailspin, leading to a rise in Treasury yields and the greenback. If the central bank chief hints that he was misunderstood, everything will be turned on its head.

Meanwhile, the strengthening of the dollar has been one of the reasons behind the yen’s fall to 40-year lows and Brent’s rapid return to pre-Middle East conflict levels. In the spring, the rise in oil prices drove the dollar higher, but now the strengthening dollar is putting downward pressure on oil prices. That said, there are already plenty of headwinds for ‘black gold’.

Since the deal with the US was concluded, Iran has exported around 40 million barrels. Coupled with the rise in Russian oil supplies to 4.13M BPD, the highest since the beginning of 2022, this is exerting significant downward pressure on Brent.

Despite flare-ups in the conflict in the Middle East, tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues. The resumption of operations there allows Goldman Sachs to forecast a 3M BPD surplus in the oil market by 2027. Of this, 1 million BPD will be used to replenish strategic reserves. However, the remaining figure will be sufficient to give crude oil bears confidence.

The only thing likely to unsettle them would be the resumption of large-scale US air strikes against Iran. Donald Trump has not ruled out such action, but favours diplomacy.

Meanwhile, the USDJPY has exceeded 162 for the first time since 1986. Investors are bracing for a resumption of currency interventions, on which Japan spent over $70 billion in April and May. According to Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, the government and the central bank are ready to take appropriate measures regarding currencies at any time as necessary. The official noted that her US counterpart, Scott Bessent, had agreed with this.

If Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra fails to halt the US dollar’s advance, Japan will have no choice but to intervene in the forex market.

Summary: A strong dollar and rising supply are weighing on Brent; Japan is preparing to intervene amid a fall in the yen. (FxPro)

News

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

On the last day of June, gold traded at $4,023 per ounce. Oversea-Chinese Banking Corporation (OCBC), a major Southeast Asian bank, lowered its year-end gold forecast from $5,100 to $4,360, citing rising real yields, a stronger U.S. dollar, and a more hawkish Federal Reserve policy. 

The bank also reduced its silver forecast by about 25% to $67. These factors push bank models downward when military conflicts fuel inflation rather than fear. 
In January, OCBC had predicted gold at $5,600. OCBC clarified that the fundamental picture for gold hasn't changed—central banks continue buying, silver supply deficits persist, and long-term outlooks remain positive. The revision reflects changed short-term conditions that no longer support the old forecast. 

The bank's January 2026 forecast assumed the Fed would continue cutting rates, the dollar would weaken, and investor demand would stay high—all of which didn't materialize. 
The U.S.-Iran conflict starting February 28 raised oil prices, triggering inflation that forced the Fed to halt monetary easing. 
According to CME FedWatch, the probability of a rate hike by September exceeds two-thirds. This boosted real yields on Treasury bonds, creating direct competition for gold, which offers no yield. OCBC's new target of $4,360 is significantly lower than other institutional forecasts. 
Goldman Sachs still sees gold at $4,900 by year-end, JPMorgan forecasts around $6,000, and Morgan Stanley predicts $5,200. None abandoned their structural bullish outlook. Goldman analysts noted that central bank gold purchases, accelerated after Russia's reserves were frozen in 2022, are structural and policy-driven, independent of Fed rate adjustments. 
These views don't contradict each other—they describe different time horizons. OCBC's revision concerns the "paper" market—how ETF flows, futures positions, and institutional investments may shift through December. 
Physical gold remains unaffected. The pressure from real yields that led OCBC to lower its target also reduces cash purchasing power on bank accounts when inflation outpaces savings rates. For metal holders, this isn't a risk but the very reason they hold gold. OCBC changed its model; the monetary outlook hasn't changed, and neither have your gold holdings.

Bottom line: At similar oil prices (~$65–$68/bbl), gasoline cost drivers $0.80 to $1.20+ less per gallon in 2018 and 2021 than right now. Gas prices track oil directionally but sit on a higher baseline today. 

https://t.co/wELuRvUtPW

Why Is Gas More Expensive Now at the Same Oil Price?Crude oil is still the largest single component (~45–55% of the pump price), but other factors have pushed the overall level higher:General inflation — Cumulative price increases since 2018 (~20–25%) and since 2021 (~15–18%) have raised labor, transportation, equipment, and operational costs across the supply chain.
Taxes — Federal excise tax is fixed (~18.4¢/gallon), but many states have raised excise taxes, sales taxes, or added environmental fees since 2018/2021.
Refining, distribution & marketing — These now make up a larger share in some periods. Refinery margins (crack spreads), maintenance, capacity utilization, and logistics costs have risen. Environmental regulations and post-pandemic supply-chain effects also play a role.
Inventory & lag effects — Recent geopolitical volatility (earlier oil spikes) left higher-cost crude and refined product in the system; it takes weeks/months for cheaper oil to fully reach the pump (“rockets and feathers” phenomenon).
Seasonal & regional factors — Summer driving demand and regional differences (e.g., West Coast often much higher) add pressure.

Visual Context Historical charts consistently show gasoline prices moving in the same direction as crude oil but with a widening gap in recent years due to the non-crude components listed above. The long-term rule of thumb (roughly $10/bbl oil change ≈ $0.25/gallon gas change) still applies directionally, but the absolute level has shifted upward. In short, $68 oil today does not buy the same cheap gas it did in 2018 or 2021 because of higher baseline costs beyond crude. As the current drop in oil fully works through the system (and if no new disruptions occur), further declines in gas prices are likely in the coming weeks and months — though probably not all the way back to 2018/2021 levels without broader cost relief.For the most precise regional or weekly data, check the EIA Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update or AAA Gas Prices. Prices vary by state due to taxes and local supply.

Indirect US-Iran Talks in Doha: Technical Progress or Strategic Stalemate?

Doha – Iran and the United States concluded another round of indirect talks in Qatar with no clear signs of progress toward a lasting peace deal, Reuters reported.

Focus on Previously “Resolved” Issues

The two-day discussions centered on maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz and the release of frozen Iranian assets — issues already addressed in an interim agreement announced two weeks ago. The critical nuclear program was not discussed.

Optimistic Statements from Washington

President Donald Trump expressed optimism, stating that “the denuclearization of Iran is moving along well.” Vice President JD Vance indicated that the nuclear issue would be addressed later. Iran, meanwhile, insisted on international recognition of its control over the strategic waterway.

Next Round After Khamenei’s Funeral

Qatar’s Foreign Ministry announced that the next meeting will take place after the funeral of Iran’s late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on July 9. The Qatari spokesperson described the talks as “positive progress” building on previous outcomes.

Economic Impact

Trump’s remarks led to a drop in oil prices to their lowest level in four months, amid ongoing tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, which handles one-fifth of global oil trade.

Despite diplomatic efforts, the path to a durable peace remains long and fraught with challenges, as differences over maritime control and the nuclear issue persist.

The drones “exploited numerous gaps” in Nato air defences during a “sustained” 15-month campaign by the Kremlin, according to a report published by the International Institute for Strategic Studies (IISS).

Among them was RAF Lakenheath, a US base in Suffolk that is preparing to host nuclear bombs. In November 2024, drones breached its airspace, along with three other US sites in England. A sanctioned Russian tanker docked in Hull at the time has since been blamed for launching the drones.

Bases across Europe were also spied on. A secret submarine base at Île Longue in Brittany, northern France, which houses the bulk of the country’s nuclear missiles, was targeted as well as air bases containing nuclear bombs in Belgium and the Netherlands. 

Analysts pinpointed the dates and times of drone sightings at military bases, airports and critical civilian infrastructure in 13 European countries between August 2024 and February 2026. On each occasion, one or more Russian shadow fleet tankers happened to be nearby.

During one incident, drones were seen deploying from a Russian spy ship before flying towards a French aircraft carrier, according to the report.

The IISS suggests this paints a picture of a widespread “coordinated” operation over Europe’s skies, making it “likely” that the Kremlin was using its sanctioned ships to deploy drones and spy on Nato’s most sensitive military locations.

“The pattern of sightings across 15 months and 13 countries cannot be explained by misidentification or opportunism alone,” said Charlie Edwards, IISS’s senior fellow for strategy and national security and a co-author of the report.

“Russia has demonstrated, repeatedly and in public, that it can penetrate the airspace of Nato member states – including over nuclear sites – without triggering a collective allied response. That gap between capability and political will is now a strategic vulnerability.”

The vessel entered the shipping lane near Dover shortly before 8am having left the Russian port of Ust-luga near the Estonian border on June 26 on its way to Singapore.

In March Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, gave the military the approval to board and seize the shadow fleet tankers in UK waters. It followed similar actions undertaken on the shadow fleet in the Mediterranean and Atlantic by French and American forces.

But Putin has continued to defy the Prime Minister, sailing more of the ships through the Channel, often guarded by Russian warships.

Current News Report — July 2, 2026

A truly complete news report cannot include every event in the world, yet the news that should be said today centers on a few heavy themes: war, heat, courts, government power, oil, AI, public safety, and human suffering that should not be ignored.

1. The biggest world story: U.S.–Iran talks and the Strait of Hormuz

The United States and Iran have concluded indirect technical talks in Doha, Qatar, focused mainly on maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and the unfreezing of Iranian funds. The talks did not appear to settle the larger nuclear issue, and no major final agreement was announced. Qatar described the talks as showing “positive progress,” yet the central dispute remains serious: Iran wants recognition of authority over the strait and the ability to collect fees from shipping, while the U.S. position is that the waterway must remain an international passage.

This matters because the Strait of Hormuz is one of the world’s most important oil chokepoints. Even small changes there can affect fuel prices, inflation, shipping, military risk, and global markets. Oil prices fell for a third straight day after the talks, with Brent near $70.91 and U.S. crude near $67.99, reflecting market hope that tensions may ease and oil flows may normalize.

What should be said: diplomacy is better than war, yet technical progress is not the same as peace. The world should watch whether the nuclear issue is addressed, whether shipping stays open, and whether either side uses the pause to prepare for another confrontation.

2. Ukraine: Russia strikes Kyiv again

Russia launched a large overnight attack on Ukraine, hitting Kyiv with missiles and drones. Reuters reported at least 13 people killed and around 90 wounded, with residential damage and renewed Ukrainian appeals for more air defense systems. Russia said the strikes targeted military, energy, and airport-related sites in retaliation for Ukrainian attacks.

What should be said: the war is not frozen, not solved, and not fading away. Civilians are still being killed. The practical issue is air defense, energy protection, and whether Ukraine’s allies will keep supplying the tools needed to reduce civilian deaths.

3. Gaza, Israel, Lebanon, and regional strain

The Israel-Hamas situation remains fragile, with Reuters’ Israel-Hamas coverage still tracking strikes, ceasefire strains, humanitarian suffering, and unresolved political questions. Recent Reuters coverage has also pointed to continuing Gaza deaths, accusations and investigations, and broader regional instability tied to Iran, Hezbollah, and Hamas.

The IMF cut Israel’s 2026 economic growth forecast from 4.8% to 3.5%, citing elevated regional tensions involving Iran, Hezbollah, and Hamas. That is not only an economic story; it shows how war damages households, public budgets, inflation, labor supply, energy costs, and long-term confidence.

What should be said: every side has political language, military language, and legal language. The human reality remains grief, displacement, fear, and exhaustion. Any honest report should keep civilians in view.

4. Europe’s deadly heat and wildfires

Europe is facing dangerous heat and wildfire conditions. Reuters reported that a record-breaking June heatwave exposed Europe’s lack of readiness for climate extremes, with temperatures above 40°C in some areas and more than 1,000 heat-related deaths estimated in Spain. The EU is working on a climate resilience plan, while most climate funding still goes to emissions reduction rather than adaptation.

In southern France, firefighters are battling multiple fires after the heatwave, including a large blaze near the Spanish border in the Aude department. Reuters reported evacuations from campsites near Fréjus and warnings of heightened wildfire risk because of heat, low humidity, and dry vegetation.

What should be said: heat is not just uncomfortable weather. It is a public-health emergency, especially for older people, children, outdoor workers, the poor, and people without safe cooling. Adaptation now matters: cooling centers, checking on neighbors, better housing insulation, shaded cities, emergency plans, and reliable power.

5. U.S. heat ahead of July 4

The United States is also dealing with major heat from Kansas to New York ahead of the July 4 holiday period. Reuters described it as a historic heatwave affecting travel, outdoor plans, agriculture, energy demand, and public health.

What should be said: holiday travel and celebration should not distract from heat safety. People need water, shade, cooling, and caution with elderly relatives, pets, children, and anyone with heart or breathing problems.

6. U.S. Supreme Court and presidential power

The U.S. Supreme Court ended its term with major rulings affecting immigration, presidential power, gun rights, transgender rights, voting law, and federal agencies. Reuters reported that the Court rejected President Trump’s bid to restrict birthright citizenship, while also issuing rulings that rewarded parts of Trump’s broader assertion of executive power.

AP reported that the Court rejected Trump’s executive order on birthright citizenship, while other rulings shaped immigration, voting, tariffs, and presidential authority.

What should be said: the legal question is not only who won each case. The deeper issue is the balance of power: Congress, the presidency, courts, states, agencies, and individual rights. Americans should pay attention to how power is being rearranged, not only to headline victories.

7. U.S. politics: money, immigration, and trust

AP reported that federal filings showed President Trump took in about $1.2 billion from crypto-related businesses last year, while political fights continued over immigration, the SAVE America Act, the Federal Reserve, Taiwan arms sales, disability-rights concerns, and other issues.

What should be said: when politics, personal wealth, crypto, federal policy, and government power overlap, citizens should ask plain questions: Who benefits? What is disclosed? What is legal? What is ethical? What serves the public rather than the powerful?

8. AI: promise and danger

A new U.N.-linked report warned that unchecked AI progress may carry catastrophic risks, while also acknowledging major potential benefits. Reuters coverage described concerns over safety, governance, inequality, misinformation, labor disruption, and misuse.

AI is also moving deeper into healthcare. Axios reported that Medicare is beginning a health-tech payment test involving more than 150 healthcare organizations, aiming to see whether AI-related tools can lower costs while improving care quality.

What should be said: AI should not be treated as magic or as a monster. It is power. It needs truthfulness, testing, human responsibility, safety rules, and humility. In medicine, law, government, education, and news, AI can help people or harm them depending on how it is governed.

9. Oil, inflation, and everyday costs

Oil prices have fallen as U.S.–Iran talks reduced immediate fear around the Strait of Hormuz, and Saudi Aramco has resumed loadings from Ras Tanura after a long disruption. Reuters reported that Aramco has moved more oil into the market, including spot sales, as Middle East flows recover.

What should be said: lower oil prices can help gasoline, shipping, food, and inflation pressure. Yet this relief depends on fragile diplomacy, shipping security, OPEC+ decisions, and whether the Middle East avoids renewed conflict.

10. Public safety and children

AP reported on a home in Hamden, Ohio, where authorities said 16 children were removed and four adults were arrested.

AP also reconstructed a deadly U.S. strike on an Iranian primary school from earlier in the year, reporting that the Feb. 28 attack killed more than 100 Iranian children and remains without a final public accounting from the Pentagon.

What should be said: children should never become footnotes. Whether the story is abuse in a home, a strike in a war zone, hunger in Gaza, heat danger in Europe, or displacement in Ukraine, the moral test of the news is whether the vulnerable are seen clearly.

11. Sports and culture

The 2026 World Cup continues to dominate sports attention. Reuters’ U.S. page noted Christian Pulisic returning to the United States starting lineup for a round-of-32 match against Bosnia, while CBS reported the U.S. defeated Bosnia 2-0 for its second-ever World Cup knockout-round win.

Reuters also reported that Canada will join the Eurovision Song Contest in 2027, a cultural story showing how global entertainment institutions continue expanding beyond their traditional boundaries.

What should be said: sports and culture are not as grave as war or heat deaths, yet they matter because people need shared moments, national identity, beauty, rest, and joy amid heavy news.

12. What is confirmed, what is uncertain, and what to watch

Confirmed: U.S.–Iran talks happened in Doha, oil prices fell, Russia struck Kyiv again, Europe and the U.S. are facing dangerous heat, the Supreme Court issued major rulings, AI governance warnings are increasing, and public-safety stories involving children remain urgent.

Uncertain: whether U.S.–Iran diplomacy will hold, whether the nuclear issue will be seriously addressed, whether Russia’s latest attacks signal a wider escalation, whether Europe’s adaptation plans will move fast enough, how the Supreme Court’s rulings will reshape government power in practice, and whether AI regulation can keep up with deployment.

Watch next: Hormuz shipping rules, oil prices, Ukraine air-defense decisions, Gaza ceasefire conditions, wildfire evacuations, heat death counts, Fed rate signals, court follow-up cases, AI safety rules, and investigations involving civilian casualties.

13. The deeper meaning of today’s news

The world is not short on information. It is short on wisdom.

The current news says that nations are powerful, yet fragile.

Technology is impressive, yet dangerous without restraint.

Courts can protect rights, yet also shift power.

Markets can calm quickly, yet panic quickly.

Heat can kill silently.

War can make children invisible unless someone keeps naming them.

The right response is not fear, cynicism, or numbness. The right response is sober attention, prayer, truthfulness, compassion, and responsibility.

A person does not need to carry the whole world alone. Yet a faithful person should refuse to look away from suffering, refuse to spread rumors, refuse to rejoice in destruction, and refuse to let the news harden the heart.

The news today should make people more prayerful, more careful, more honest, and more merciful.

🛢️ Oil Erases Its Geopolitical Premium — Is the Downtrend Just Beginning?

Oil prices have fallen back to levels seen before the US-Iran conflict, with Brent crude now trading near $70 per barrel as markets fully price out the geopolitical risk premium.

The rapid decline has been even faster than the normalization seen after the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict or the 1990 Gulf War.

📉 Why Oil Is Falling

Several factors are weighing on prices:

• The temporary US-Iran ceasefire has eased fears over the Strait of Hormuz.
• Global oil flows are gradually returning to normal.
• US crude production is approaching 14 million barrels per day, a record high.
• OPEC+ continues to increase production quotas.
• China's economic slowdown is limiting demand growth.
The International Energy Agency (IEA) has also cut its 2026 global oil demand forecast, reinforcing the bearish outlook.
⚠️ But Risks Haven't Disappeared
Despite the recent selloff, several structural risks remain:
• The US-Iran agreement is only a 60-day memorandum.
• Strategic oil reserves remain near multi-decade lows.
• Commercial inventories are still below historical averages.
• Any disruption in the Strait of Hormuz could quickly send prices higher again.

📊 Technical Outlook

Brent has erased nearly all of its war premium and is testing a major support area around $70.

If selling pressure continues, traders will be watching the $62–65 zone as the next major demand area.

📌 Key Takeaway
Markets have quickly shifted from pricing geopolitical risk to focusing on supply growth and weaker demand.
However, with inventories still tight and the Middle East situation unresolved, oil could remain one of the most volatile assets during the second half of 2026.
